sources.title: OCR A Level Mathematics A H240 specification

statements.topicHeading: Content of A Level in Mathematics A
statements.statementText: This A Level qualification builds on the skills, knowledge and understanding set out in the whole GCSE (9–1) subject content for mathematics for first teaching from 2015. All of this content is assumed, but will only be explicitly assessed where it appears in this specification.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 9, line IDs p9-r2-b1-l0,p9-r2-b1-l1,p9-r2-b1-l2,p9-r2-b1-l3,p9-r2-b1-l4,p9-r2-b1-l5, 2a-assumed

statements.statementText: Learners are expected to be able to use their knowledge to reason mathematically and solve problems both within mathematics and in context. Content that is covered by any statement may be required in problem solving, modelling and reasoning tasks even if that is not explicitly stated in the statement.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 9, line IDs p9-r6-b2-l0,p9-r6-b2-l1,p9-r6-b2-l2,p9-r6-b2-l3,p9-r6-b2-l4,p9-r6-b2-l5,p9-r6-b2-l6, 2a-reasoning

statements.statementText: The large data set (LDS) is a pre-released set or sets of data that should be used as teaching material throughout the course. This data set will be made available on the OCR website, along with a document giving the source(s) and associate metadata, and will remain for the life of the specification, unless the review process identifies a necessary change. Any change to the data set will be made before the beginning of any given two year course and centres will be notified a year in advance.

statements.statementText: The purpose of the LDS is that learners experience working with real data in the classroom and explore this data using appropriate technology. It is principally intended to enrich the teaching and learning of statistics, through which learners will become familiar with the context and main features of the data.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 10, line IDs p10-r4-b0-l0,p10-r4-b0-l1,p10-r4-b0-l2,p10-r4-b0-l3,p10-r4-b0-l4,p10-r4-b0-l5,p10-r4-b0-l6, 2b-lds-purpose

statements.statementText: The use of the word ‘exact’ also tells learners that rigorous (exact) working is expected in the answer to the question.
notesText: Coordinate-extraction fragments are retained in source accounting but omitted from display.
examplesText: ln 2 + ln 3 − ln 1 should be written as ln 6.
Find the exact solution of ln x = 2. The correct answer is e² and not 7.389 056.
Find the exact solution of 3x = 2. The correct answer is x = 2/3 or x = 0.6̇, not x = 0.67 or similar.

statements.statementText: Prove Learners are given a statement and must provide a formal mathematical argument which demonstrates its validity. A formal proof requires a high level of mathematical detail, with candidates clearly defining variables, correct algebraic manipulation and a concise conclusion.
examplesText:
Example Question
Prove that the sum of the squares of any three consecutive positive integers cannot be divided by 3.
Example Response
Let the three consecutive positive integers be n, n + 1 and n + 2.
n² + (n + 1)² + (n + 2)² = 3n² + 6n + 5
= 3(n² + 2n + 1) + 2
This always leaves a remainder of 2 and so cannot be divided by 3.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 13, line IDs p13-r0-b0-l0,p13-r0-b1-l0,p13-r0-b1-l1,p13-r0-b2-l0,p13-r0-b2-l1,p13-r2-b0-l0,p13-r2-b1-l0,p13-r2-b1-l1,p13-r2-b2-l0,p13-r2-b3-l0,p13-r2-b4-l0,p13-r2-b5-l0,p13-r2-b5-l1,p13-r2-b6-l0,p13-r2-b6-l1,p13-r2-b7-l0, p13-command-prove

statements.statementText: Show that Learners are given a result and have to show that it is true. Because they are given the result, the explanation has to be sufficiently detailed to cover every step of their working.
examplesText:
Example Question
Show that the curve y = x ln x has a stationary point (1/e, -1/e).
Example Response
dy/dx = 1·ln x + x·(1/x) = ln x + 1
dy/dx = 0 for stationary point
When x = 1/e, dy/dx = ln(1/e) + 1 = -1 + 1 = 0, so stationary.
When x = 1/e, y = (1/e) ln(1/e) = -1/e, so (1/e, -1/e) is a stationary point on the curve.

statements.statementText: Determine This command word indicates that justification should be given for any results found, including working where appropriate.
examplesText:
Example Question
Determine the coordinates of the stationary point of the curve y = x ln x .
Example Response
dy/dx = 1·ln x + x·(1/x) = ln x + 1
ln x + 1 = 0 ⇒ x = 0.368...
When x = 0.368..., y = 0.368... × ln(0.368...) = -0.368...
So (0.368, –0.368)

examplesText: Example Question Find the coordinates of the stationarty point of the curve y = x ln x . Example Response (0.368, –0.368)
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 14, line IDs p14-r2-b3-l0,p14-r2-b4-l0,p14-r2-b4-l1,p14-r2-b4-l2,p14-r2-b5-l0,p14-r2-b6-l0,p14-r2-b7-l0,p14-r2-b8-l0, p14-command-find-solve-calculate

statements.statementText: Verify A clear substitution of the given value to justify the statement is required.
examplesText:
Example Question
Verify that the curve y = x ln x has a stationarty point at x = 1/e.
Example Response
dy/dx = ln x + 1
At x = 1/e, dy/dx = ln(1/e) + 1 = -1 + 1 = 0, therefore it is a stationarty point.

statements.topicHeading: In this question you must show detailed reasoning
statements.statementText: In this question you must show detailed reasoning. When a question includes this instruction learners must give a solution which leads to a conclusion showing a detailed and complete analytical method. Their solution should contain sufficient detail to allow the line of their argument to be followed. This is not a restriction on a learner’s use of a calculator when tackling the question, e.g. for checking an answer or evaluating a function at a given point, but it is a restriction on what will be accepted as evidence of a complete method.
notesText: In these examples variations in the structure of the answers are possible, for example using a different base for the logarithms in example 1, and different intermediate steps may be given.
examplesText:
Example 1:
Use logarithms to solve the equation 3²ˣ⁺¹ = 4¹⁰⁰, giving your answer correct to 3 significant figures.
The answer is x = 62.6, but the learner must include the steps log 3²ˣ⁺¹ = log 4¹⁰⁰, (2x + 1) log 3 = log 4¹⁰⁰ and an intermediate evaluation step, for example 2x + 1 = 126.18… . Using the solve function on a calculator to skip one of these steps would not result in a complete analytical method.

Example 2:
Evaluate ∫₀¹ (x³ + 4x² − 1) dx.
The answer is 7/12, but the learner must include at least [¼x⁴ + ⁴⁄₃x³ − x]₀¹ and the substitution ¼ + ⁴⁄₃ − 1. Just writing down the answer using the definite integral function on a calculator would therefore not be awarded any marks.

Example 3:
Solve the equation 3 sin 2x = cos x for 0° ≤ x ≤ 180°.
The answer is x = 9.59°, 90° or 170° (to 3sf), but the learner must include … 6 sin x cos x − cos x = 0, cos x(6 sin x − 1) = 0, cos x = 0 or sin x = 1/6.
A graphical method which investigated the intersections of the curves y = 3 sin 2x and y = cos x would be acceptable to find the solution at 90° if carefully verified, but the other two solutions must be found analytically, not numerically.

statements.statementText: Give, State, Write down These command words indicate that neither working nor justification is required.

statements.statementText: Hence When a question uses the word ‘hence’, it is an indication that the next step should be based on what has gone before. The intention is that learners should start from the indicated statement.
examplesText: You are given that f(x) = 2x³ − x² − 7x + 6. Show that (x − 1) is a factor of f(x).
Hence find the three factors of f(x).
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 16, line IDs p16-r0-b0-l0,p16-r0-b1-l0,p16-r0-b1-l1,p16-r0-b2-l0,p16-r2-b0-l0, p16-command-hence

statements.statementText: Hence or otherwise This is used when there are multiple ways of answering a given question. Learners starting from the indicated statement may well gain some information about the solution from doing so, and may already be some way towards the answer. The command phrase is used to direct learners towards using a particular piece of information to start from or to a particular method. It also indicates to learners that valid alternate methods exist which will be given full credit, but that they may be more time-consuming or complex.
examplesText: Example:
Show that (cos x + sin x)² = 1 + sin 2x for all x.
Hence or otherwise, find the derivative of (cos x + sin x)².
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 16, line IDs p16-r2-b1-l0,p16-r2-b2-l0,p16-r2-b2-l1,p16-r2-b2-l2,p16-r2-b2-l3,p16-r2-b2-l4,p16-r2-b3-l0,p16-r2-b4-l0,p16-r2-b5-l0, p16-command-hence-or-otherwise

examplesText: Example:

statements.statementText: Be able to manipulate polynomials algebraically.
notesText: Includes expanding brackets, collecting like terms, factorising, simple algebraic division and use of the factor theorem. Learners should be familiar with the terms ‘quadratic’, ‘cubic’ and ‘parabola’. Learners should be familiar with the factor theorem as: 1. f(a) = 0 ⇔ (x − a) is a factor of f(x); 2. f(b/a) = 0 ⇔ (ax − b) is a factor of f(x). They should be able to use the factor theorem to find a linear factor of a polynomial normally of degree ≤ 3. They may also be required to find factors of a polynomial, using any valid method, e.g. by inspection.

statements.statementText: Understand and be able to use the definition of a function.
notesText: The vocabulary and associated notation is expected i.e. the terms many-one, one-many, one-one, mapping, image, range, domain. Includes knowing that a function is a mapping from the domain to the range such that for each x in the domain, there is a unique y in the range with f(x)=y. The range is the set of all possible values of f(x); learners are expected to use set notation where appropriate.

statements.statementText: a) Understand and be able to use the language of statistical hypothesis testing, developed through a binomial model: null hypothesis, alternative hypothesis, significance level, test statistic, 1-tail test, 2-tail test, critical value, critical region, acceptance region, p-value.
notesText: Hypotheses should be stated in terms of parameter values (where relevant) and the meanings of symbols should be stated. For example, ‘H₀: p = 0.7, H₁: p ≠ 0.7, where p is the population proportion in favour of the resolution’. Conclusions should be stated in such a way as to reflect the fact that they are not certain.
examplesText: “ H 0 : p = 0.7 , H 1 : p ! 0.7 , where p is the population proportion in favour of the resolution”.
“There is evidence at the 5% level to reject H 0 . It is likely that the mean mass is less than 500 g.”
“There is no evidence at the 2% level to reject H 0 . There is no reason to suppose that the mean journey time has changed.”
“ H 0 is rejected. Waiting times have increased.”
“Accept H 0 . Plants in this area have the same height as plants in other areas.”

reviewNotes: Covers formulation of null and alternative hypotheses.
Concerns one-tailed and two-tailed tests.
Significance level and p-value are core to significance testing.

reviewNotes: Codex residual seed (knowledge-v5-residual-codex-resolution-1): Applying Newton's second law in vector component form for 2D particle dynamics is a distinct skill not represented by any single existing leaf; the component skills exist but the combination requires a new leaf.

statements.statementText: Understand the concept of a frictional force and be able to apply it in contexts where the force is given in vector or component form, or the magnitude and direction of the force are given.

reviewNotes: Core concept of friction.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: given in vector or component form
reviewNotes: Applying force as a vector to friction problems.
Resolving friction into components when needed.

statements.statementText: Be able to represent the contact force between two rough surfaces by two components (the ‘normal’ contact force and the ‘frictional’ contact force).
notesText: Questions set will explicitly use the terms normal (contact) force, frictional (contact) force and magnitude of the contact force.

reviewNotes: Explicitly refers to the normal component.
Explicitly refers to the frictional component.
The contact force is resolved into two perpendicular components.

statements.statementText: Understand and be able to use the coefficient of friction and the F ≤ μR model of friction in one and two dimensions, including the concept of limiting friction.
notesText: [Knowledge of the angle of friction is excluded.]
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 70, line IDs p70-r16-b0-l0,p70-r16-b0-l1,p70-r16-b0-l2,p70-r16-b0-l3,p70-r17-b0-l0, OFFICIAL-OCR-H240-3.03t

reviewNotes: The node defines the coefficient of friction, which is explicitly named in the statement.
The statement lists limiting friction as a concept to understand.
The model F ≤ μR is a quantitative description of frictional force.
The model uses normal reaction R; understanding it is essential.
The node covers applying Newton's laws with friction, which matches the statement's scope of using the model in dimensions.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: Understand and be able to solve problems regarding the static equilibrium of a body on a rough surface and solve problems regarding limiting equilibrium.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: Understand and be able to solve problems regarding the static equilibrium of a body on a rough surface and solve problems regarding limiting equilibrium.

statements.statementText: Understand and be able to solve problems regarding the motion of a body on a rough surface.
notesText: [Problems set on inclined planes will only consider motion along the line of greatest slope and therefore a vector consideration of the motion will not be required.]
examplesText: e.g. The motion of a body projected down a line of greatest slope on a rough inclined plane.

statements.statementText: a) Be able to calculate the moment of a force about an axis through a point in the plane of the body.
notesText: For coplanar forces, moments may be described as being about a point. [Understanding of the vector nature of moments is excluded.]
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 71, line IDs p71-r10-b0-l0,p71-r10-b0-l1,p71-r11-b0-l0,p71-r11-b0-l1,p71-r11-b1-l0,p71-r11-b1-l1,p71-r6-b0-l0, OFFICIAL-OCR-H240-3.04a

reviewNotes: The node is the definition and calculation of moments of forces, which matches the statement exactly.
Resolving a force into components is often used to determine the perpendicular distance for moment calculation.

statements.statementText: b) Understand that when a rigid body is in equilibrium the resultant moment is zero and the resultant force is zero.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 71, line IDs p71-r12-b0-l0,p71-r13-b0-l0,p71-r13-b0-l1,p71-r13-b0-l2, OFFICIAL-OCR-H240-3.04b

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: when a rigid body is in equilibrium the resultant moment is zero and the resultant force is zero
reviewNotes: The node defines these exact conditions for rigid body equilibrium.
The principle of equilibrium covers only the force condition, which is part of the statement.
Understanding moment is necessary to appreciate the moment condition.
Newton's first law gives the condition for translational equilibrium, which aligns with the force statement.

statements.statementText: c) Be able to use moments in simple static contexts.
notesText: Questions will be set in which the context of the problem can be modelled using rectangular laminas, uniform and non-uniform rods only. Learners may assume that: 1. for a uniform rod the weight acts at the midpoint of the rod, 2. for a non-uniform rod the weight acts at either a specified given point or is to be determined by moments, 3. for a rectangular lamina the weight acts at its point of symmetry.
examplesText: e.g. To determine the forces acting on a horizontal beam or to determine the forces acting on a ladder resting on horizontal ground against a vertical wall.

reviewNotes: The statement is about applying moment calculations in static contexts.
Equilibrium conditions are the basis for static analysis, including moments.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: for a uniform rod the weight acts at the midpoint
reviewNotes: Centre of mass determines where weight acts, which is crucial for moment calculations in these problems.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: ladder resting on horizontal ground against a vertical wall
reviewNotes: Friction is present in typical ladder problems, a common static context.
Normal reaction is a contact force present in these static configurations.
Equilibrium of forces is a prerequisite for solving static problems.
